talk_title: Risk for Pediatric Cancer in Newborn Screening

models.name: Michigan newborn blood spot cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 2000 children with linked tumor outcomes, 6.8% pathogenic variants

models.name: NCARE prospective newborn screening trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: target 10,000 newborns, focus on RB1, WT1, DICER1

clinical_data.metric: cancer death reduction for predisposition carriers
estimated cancer risk for positive blood spot

MOA_summary: Newborn screening using targeted NGS panels identifies pathogenic variants in cancer predisposition genes (e.g., RB1, TP53) from dried blood spots, enabling early surveillance and treatment to reduce mortality and treatment-related toxicity in high‑risk children.
key_findings: Modeling predicts newborn cancer‑risk screening could reduce deaths before age 20 by 8% and by up to 54% in carriers of predisposition syndromes.

Retrospective analysis of 2,000 Michigan newborn spots found 6.8% carried pathogenic variants, with 5/5 RET carriers developing medullary thyroid cancer and RB1 carriers developing retinoblastoma or pinealoblastoma.
Estimated incidence of cancer among newborns with a positive screen is ~1 in 30,000, comparable to other rare screened conditions.
Prospective NCARE trial will randomize 10,000 mothers to focused vs. broad panels to assess feasibility, acceptability, and downstream clinical impact.
open_questions: What proportion of newborns with a positive genetic screen will never develop cancer (penetrance uncertainty)?
How to balance panel breadth with cost and false‑positive rates in a public health setting?

talk_title: Therapeutic Precision Cancer Prevention: The Power of Canaries

models.name: CAP2 (Lynch syndrome aspirin trial)
models.genotype_or_key_features: MSH2/MLH1 carriers, 4‑year aspirin, follow‑up to 10 years

models.name: CAP3 dose‑finding aspirin trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: 1,547 Lynch syndrome carriers, 100 mg, 300 mg, 600 mg aspirin arms

clinical_data.metric: hazard ratio for all Lynch‑syndrome cancers (CAP3 vs historic placebo)
incidence reduction for upper GI cancers (CAP2)
serious adverse events in 100 mg aspirin arm

MOA_summary: Low‑dose aspirin reduces cancer incidence in Lynch syndrome by inhibiting platelet‑derived thromboxane A2, thereby enhancing anti‑tumor T‑cell responses; dose‑finding trials (CAP3) show the smallest dose (100 mg) yields the fewest cancers with minimal toxicity.
key_findings: CAP3 trial (1,547 participants) demonstrated the lowest cancer incidence in the 100 mg aspirin arm, with 30% of participants experiencing the fewest cancers after 8 years.
Serious adverse events were absent in the 100 mg group, indicating a favorable safety profile compared with higher doses.
Combined analysis of CAP2 and CAP3 yields HR 0.67 versus historic placebo, confirming a protective effect of aspirin across Lynch‑syndrome cancer sites.
Guideline update in the UK will recommend ~81 mg aspirin starting five years before the first colonoscopy for Lynch carriers.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Aspirin efficacy appears dose‑dependent with lower doses outperforming higher doses, suggesting a narrow therapeutic window.
Obesity may require dose doubling, indicating metabolic factors influence aspirin’s preventive effect.

combination_strategies.rationale: Aspirin inactivates platelet COX‑1, reducing thromboxane A2‑mediated T‑cell inhibition, synergizing with vaccine‑induced immunity
combination_strategies.phenotype: enhanced tumor‑specific T‑cell responses and reduced cancer incidence

open_questions: What is the optimal aspirin dose for obese Lynch carriers?
Can aspirin be combined with emerging MSI‑plus assay‑guided surveillance to further lower cancer risk?

talk_title: Vaccines for Cancer Prevention and Intervention

biomarkers.name: type‑1 T‑cell response
biomarkers.finding: 100% of NAUS209 recipients generated CD8+ T‑cell responses to at least one frameshift antigen

biomarkers.name: antibody titers against HER2
biomarkers.finding: Significant increase in HER2‑specific IgG in >70% of DCIS vaccine recipients

models.name: NAUS209 Lynch syndrome vaccine
models.genotype_or_key_features: 45 Lynch syndrome patients with high‑grade dysplasia, phase I

models.name: TgMMTV‑PyM obesity‑breast cancer model
models.genotype_or_key_features: Obese mice, Advac vaccine vs sham

clinical_data.metric: immune response rate to frameshift antigens
pathologic complete response in HER2‑positive DCIS

MOA_summary: Vaccines deliver tumor‑associated antigens (e.g., HER2, MUC1, frameshift neoantigens) via dendritic cell loading or viral vectors to elicit robust type‑1 CD4⁺/CD8⁺ T‑cell and antibody responses that can eradicate pre‑invasive lesions (interception) or provide prophylactic immunity in high‑risk individuals such as Lynch syndrome carriers.
key_findings: HER2/neu dendritic‑cell vaccination induced type‑1 T‑cell and antibody responses and led to loss of HER2 expression in 7/11 evaluable DCIS patients.
Pathologic complete responses were observed in ~30% of HER2‑positive, ER‑negative DCIS patients receiving the vaccine.
NAUS209 (viral‑vector frameshift vaccine) was safe (8% grade 3 AEs) and immunogenic, with 100% of 45 Lynch syndrome participants generating T‑cell responses, persisting in 85% at 12 months.

Adipocyte‑directed Advac vaccine reduced visceral CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration, restored regulatory T‑cells, improved insulin sensitivity, and prevented tumor development in 60% of obese breast‑cancer‑prone mice.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: HER2 loss after vaccination suggests antigen escape as a potential resistance mechanism.
Obesity‑driven vaccine targets metabolic antigens, addressing a novel selective vulnerability.

combination_strategies.rationale: Aspirin may enhance vaccine‑induced immunity by inhibiting platelet‑derived immunosuppression
combination_strategies.phenotype: synergistic tumor regression in preclinical models

open_questions: Will prophylactic vaccination in Lynch syndrome reduce cancer incidence long‑term?
What is the optimal dosing schedule for anti‑inflammatory vaccines in obese populations?

talk_title: AI in Cancer Diagnosis: Promise, Performance, and the Human‑AI Factor

models.name: PRISM mammography AI trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: 400,000 screening exams, randomization to AI‑assisted vs standard reading

models.name: Pathology AI variability study
models.genotype_or_key_features: 240 cases, 187 pathologists, repeat reads after 6‑month washout

clinical_data.metric: recall rate with AI in mammography (PRISM)
pathologist concordance with expert consensus (skin biopsy)

MOA_summary: AI models process imaging (mammography, pathology slides) to generate diagnostic predictions; variability studies reveal human inter‑observer inconsistency, while AI can improve reproducibility but may introduce automation bias, highlighting the need for rigorous evaluation and human‑in‑the‑loop designs.
key_findings: In a large mammography cohort, AI assistance was associated with a statistically significant increase in recall rate, suggesting potential over‑diagnosis.
Only 10% of 36 pathologists agreed with expert consensus on a challenging skin biopsy, illustrating high diagnostic variability.
Repeated reads by the same pathologists after six months showed poor intra‑observer reproducibility, underscoring the need for decision support.
Human‑AI interaction studies (PRISM) are enrolling 400,000 exams to assess real‑world impact of AI cue timing and presentation.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Automation bias may cause clinicians to over‑rely on AI outputs, increasing false‑positive recalls.
De‑skilling risk: reduced independent diagnostic skill with prolonged AI use.

combination_strategies.rationale: Combining AI predictions with expert oversight may mitigate automation bias and improve reproducibility
combination_strategies.phenotype: maintained accuracy with reduced recall rates in pilot studies

open_questions: What is the optimal timing and format of AI cues to minimize automation bias?
How can AI training datasets be diversified to avoid racial and demographic bias?
